blurb: Indexing, sharding, replication, consistency, SQL vs NoSQL.

questions.q: A query needs to return rows ordered by (created_at DESC). Which index is best?
choices: B-tree on created_at with DESC order
No index — ORDER BY is always fast
questions.explanation: B-tree supports range queries AND sorted iteration — critical for ORDER BY. Hash indexes only support equality lookups. For descending, some engines need an explicit DESC index.

questions.q: You're choosing between hash sharding and range sharding for a time-series workload. Which creates more hotspots?
choices: Neither — time series needs no sharding
questions.explanation: Range sharding by timestamp puts ALL recent writes on the latest shard — classic hotspot. Hash sharding spreads writes, but loses range-query locality. The tradeoff is always in play.

questions.q: In a Postgres write-ahead-log (WAL), why does group commit improve throughput?
choices: Batches many commits into one fsync, amortizing the disk-flush cost
questions.explanation: Each fsync costs 1–10ms. Committing 100 transactions in one fsync beats 100 individual fsyncs. Trade: durability window is bounded by commit interval, typically 5–10ms.

choices: A latency-vs-consistency tradeoff when no partition exists
questions.explanation: PACELC: during a Partition, choose Availability or Consistency; Else, choose Latency or Consistency. Dynamo is PA/EL (always latency). Spanner is PC/EC (always consistency).

questions.q: Quorum read/write with N=3 replicas. If R=2 and W=2, is consistency guaranteed?
questions.explanation: R+W > N guarantees overlap between readers and latest writer. N=3, R=2, W=2 → overlap. Dynamo's default is exactly this.

questions.q: LSM trees vs B-trees: which is generally faster for write-heavy workloads?
questions.explanation: LSM: writes go to an in-memory buffer + sequential WAL, then flushed as sorted SSTables. No random-write amplification. Read amp is higher (check multiple SSTables). RocksDB/Cassandra/LevelDB are LSM.

questions.q: A multi-tenant SaaS database uses one schema-per-tenant vs one row-per-tenant. What's the main operational downside of schema-per-tenant?
choices: Schema migrations must apply to every tenant schema
questions.explanation: Schema-per-tenant: 10K tenants = 10K migrations per deploy. Shared-schema with tenant_id is simpler to migrate but weaker isolation. Mid-tier: schema groups or DB-per-tenant for large customers.

questions.q: Which type of replication has the WORST RPO (potential data loss window) in exchange for the lowest write latency?
questions.explanation: Async replication: writes ack immediately; replication lag can be seconds. If primary crashes, unreplicated writes are lost. Sync replication ensures zero loss but adds RTT to every write.

questions.q: Your MySQL primary has 5 seconds of replication lag to its read replicas. A user updates their profile photo, then immediately loads their profile page (served by a replica). They see the old photo. What's the standard fix?
choices: Read-your-own-writes: route reads for recently-written data to the primary for a short window, then fall back to replicas
questions.explanation: Read-your-own-writes consistency: after a write, the session routes reads for that entity to the primary for a bounded period (e.g., 10 seconds). Other users can tolerate eventual consistency from replicas. This avoids paying the cost of reading everything from the primary.

questions.q: During an AWS us-east-1 outage, your DynamoDB global table has replicas in us-west-2 and eu-west-1. You failover reads and writes to us-west-2. When us-east-1 recovers, what conflict scenario must you handle?
choices: Last-writer-wins conflicts from writes that were in-flight to us-east-1 before the partition, now replicating back
All data in us-east-1 is lost
questions.explanation: DynamoDB global tables use last-writer-wins (by timestamp) for conflict resolution. Writes accepted by us-east-1 before it went down but not yet replicated may conflict with writes to us-west-2 during failover. The later timestamp wins, potentially overwriting valid data.

questions.q: A PostgreSQL query that used to take 5ms now takes 2 seconds. EXPLAIN shows a sequential scan on a 50M-row table instead of the expected index scan. The index exists. What's the most likely cause?
choices: Table statistics are stale; the planner estimated fewer rows than reality and chose a bad plan
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL's query planner relies on pg_statistic. If ANALYZE hasn't run after significant data changes, the planner's row estimates are wrong, causing it to pick a seq scan over an index scan. Running ANALYZE or tuning autovacuum settings fixes this.

questions.q: Your sharded MongoDB cluster uses user_id as the shard key. A new feature introduces an admin dashboard that queries across ALL users with a date range filter. What problem will you hit?
choices: Query is fast because user_id is indexed
Scatter-gather: the query must fan out to every shard since it doesn't filter by shard key, causing high latency
questions.explanation: Queries that don't include the shard key become scatter-gather (broadcast to all shards, merge results). This is O(shards) latency. The fix is either a secondary index/collection sharded by date, or a separate analytics store (CQRS pattern) for cross-shard queries.

questions.q: You run a two-node PostgreSQL cluster with synchronous replication. The standby node crashes. What happens to write transactions on the primary?
choices: Writes block indefinitely waiting for the standby to acknowledge, causing a full production outage
Writes are redirected to a new standby
Writes are buffered in WAL and sent later
questions.explanation: Synchronous replication requires at least one standby to ACK before the primary commits. With the only standby down, every COMMIT hangs. This is why production setups use synchronous_standby_names with multiple standbys or 'ANY 1' of N, so losing one standby doesn't block writes.
